primary_diagnosis.name: Actinic keratosis with acute traumatic inflammation and minor haemorrhage (on a background of field cancerisation)

pathophysiology_summary: Chronic UV exposure produces keratinocyte dysplasia — actinic keratoses — across a sun-damaged field. AKs are premalignant and a minority progress to invasive squamous cell carcinoma. Here, minor trauma to one hyperkeratotic scalp AK caused focal inflammation and bleeding, exaggerated by apixaban anticoagulation. The acute change is traumatic rather than a malignant transformation, evidenced by absence of a firm indurated base, rapid nodular growth, deep ulceration, or lymphadenopathy. However, an AK that is painful, indurated, rapidly enlarging or ulcerating warrants biopsy to exclude SCC — so the correct output is haemostasis + reassurance PLUS a definite biopsy/skin-check pathway, not dismissal.

differential.evidence_basis: Chronic rough patch on sun-damaged scalp, acute change after touch/trauma, on anticoagulant.
AK can transform to SCC; a painful/indurated/rapidly changing lesion needs biopsy to exclude.
Scalp/face BCC possible, but no pearly rolled edge or arborising vessels described.
Consider if purulence/spreading warmth — none present.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Keep SCC as important-not-to-miss and arrange biopsy/skin check; do not label definitively benign remotely, and do not escalate to emergency for uncomplicated minor bleeding.

differential.evidence_basis: No induration, rapid nodular growth, deep ulcer or nodes; bleeding explained by apixaban.
Cannot exclude remotely — biopsy of the symptomatic lesion is required.
No purulence, spreading erythema or fluctuance.

differential.diagnosis: Traumatised actinic keratosis (rule out SCC on biopsy)
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis: haemostasis + urgent skin-check/biopsy pathway; sun protection and field therapy.

red_flags.description: Firm indurated base, rapid nodular growth, or deep non-healing ulceration (SCC features)
red_flags.clinical_significance: These would indicate invasive SCC needing urgent excision; absent here but must be actively sought and biopsy arranged.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed SCC can invade and metastasise.

red_flags.description: Uncontrolled bleeding on anticoagulation not stopping with 15 min direct pressure
red_flags.clinical_significance: On apixaban, persistent bleeding can be significant and require ED haemostasis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Ongoing blood loss / need for procedural haemostasis.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Escalating to emergency for minor traumatic bleeding of a benign-appearing AK.
Declaring the lesion definitely benign remotely and not arranging biopsy, missing possible SCC.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Definitive exclusion of SCC without histology
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Direct pressure for haemostasis and a non-adherent dressing; arrange urgent (7–14 day) in-person skin check/biopsy; ED only if bleeding is uncontrolled.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Remote reassurance without a biopsy pathway risks missing SCC; emergency escalation for minor bleeding is over-triage.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Cancer Council Australia keratinocyte cancer guidance
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Suspicious, symptomatic, indurated or non-healing keratotic lesions should be biopsied; manage field cancerisation and emphasise sun protection.
